entry.title: What is SAP plant reallocation?
entry.content:
If you’ve worked in SAP transformation for long enough, you quickly realise that organisational structures rarely stay static for very long. Companies acquire new businesses. They divest underperforming ones. Manufacturing footprints change. Supply chains get redesigned. Tax structures evolve. Geopolitical events suddenly make one region less attractive than another.

Over the past few years, those changes have arguably accelerated even further as organisations respond to inflation, supply chain disruption, rising labour costs and geopolitical uncertainty. In many cases, businesses are being forced to rethink where they manufacture products, where they hold inventory and how their operational footprint is structured.

This often creates a very specific SAP challenge. A business may decide to move manufacturing from one country to another, consolidate multiple production facilities into one location or transfer responsibility for a plant from one legal entity to another. From a business perspective, this may seem like a relatively straightforward organisational change. From an SAP perspective, however, it can be significantly more complicated.

Enter the SAP plant reallocation project.

What is SAP plant reallocation?

At a high level, SAP plant reallocation refers to moving an existing plant from one company code to another, following some kind of organisational restructuring. While this sounds relatively simple on paper, the challenge is that plants sit at the centre of many critical SAP processes and are heavily embedded within both logistics and finance functions.

A plant is typically linked to material master records, inventory, production orders, purchase orders, sales orders, valuation records, warehouse processes and financial postings. Over time, that plant builds up a significant amount of transactional history and operational dependencies. This means organisations cannot simply change a configuration setting and move on. They need a way to move the plant without breaking operational processes, losing historical data or creating major disruption for the business.

Why do organisations need plant reallocation?

The reasons for undertaking plant reallocations vary. In some cases, organisations are moving manufacturing operations to lower-cost regions to improve profitability. In others, businesses are redesigning supply chains to reduce dependency on specific geographies after experiencing disruptions in recent years.

Mergers and acquisitions can also create scenarios where newly acquired manufacturing locations need to be aligned to existing legal structures. Similarly, divestitures may require certain plants to move into new legal entities while others remain with the original business. Tax, regulatory and customs changes can also force businesses to rethink how their operational footprint is structured.

Regardless of the reason, the SAP challenge tends to remain the same: how do you move a plant from one company code to another without causing unnecessary operational disruption?

Why is this difficult in SAP?

This is where things become technically challenging. Once a plant has been active for a long period of time, it becomes connected to a wide range of master and transactional data. This can include stock, open purchase orders, open production orders, batch records, warehouse management data, valuation records, financial postings and reporting structures. Organisations also need to think carefully about historical reporting, audit requirements and financial continuity. Even if a plant moves to a new legal entity, the business still needs access to historical transactions and reporting data.

The traditional approach

Typically, many organisations have handled this challenge using a manual approach. This often involves freezing activity in the original plant, creating a new plant under the new company code and then manually recreating the relevant master data and transactional objects. Inventory may need to be transferred manually, open transactions may need to be recreated and finance teams often need to perform significant reconciliation work to ensure reporting remains accurate.

While this approach can work, it is time-consuming, it introduces operational downtime and creates significant manual effort. It can also introduce unnecessary risk, particularly when large volumes of transactions need to be recreated manually. And from the perspective of the business user, it creates a break between the transactional activity in the new company and the historical activity in the previous company code.

The table-based transformation approach

A more modern approach involves transforming the underlying SAP data model at table level rather than manually rebuilding everything from scratch. Instead of creating a completely new plant and migrating operational activity manually, organisations can use landscape transformation tools to update the relevant SAP tables so that the existing plant reflects the new organisational structure.

This approach typically involves updating plant assignments, company code relationships, valuation records and other dependent logistics and finance objects. Because these objects are often deeply interconnected, this requires a structured and controlled transformation process. This is where specialist tools such as Natuvion Data Conversion Suite (DCS) are often used.

Landscape transformation technology enables organisations to analyse the impacted data objects, identify dependencies between tables and execute the required transformation in a controlled way, at scale and at pace. Rather than manually rebuilding operational processes, the software helps organisations transform existing structures while preserving transactional history and reducing manual effort. You might think of this as business redesign from the inside out.

This can significantly reduce downtime, minimise disruption to the business and allow organisations to adapt far more quickly when operational changes are required.

Final thoughts

Plant reallocation in SAP is not something organisations should underestimate. Even with specialist transformation tooling, careful planning is required to understand which data objects are impacted, how integrations may be affected and what historical information needs to remain accessible after the move. Testing is also critical to ensure operational processes continue functioning as expected once the transformation has been completed.

Plant reallocation may not be discussed as frequently as carve-outs, cloud moves or S/4HANA migrations, but I suspect it will become increasingly common as businesses continue adapting their operational footprint in response to changing market conditions. As organisations become more agile, their SAP landscapes need to become more flexible as well. The traditional manual approach can still work, but table-based transformation approaches are increasingly offering organisations a quicker and less disruptive way to respond to change.

If you would like to read more on this topic, you can do so here.

entry.title: ABAP Versions Explorer
entry.content:
There are several ways to check and compare code versions for SAP ABAP: the old classic method in the SAP GUI, a very good option in Eclipse, and the industry standard in AbapGit or on GitHub. These are all useful, but some activities are monotonous and take more time than desired.

 

Therefore, a new program Abap Versions Eplorer was developed with faster access to modified objects by program/include/FM, class, TR/TASK, or package, with multiple display and filtering options. An additional "Who is Blame" feature was also implemented to identify the actual authors of the changes.

Pick one of the available object types to view its versions. Input the name and press Enter. As this is a multi-window application, pressing Enter again will launch a new window. You can open as many windows as your monitor can handle without getting claustrophobic ).

There are several options for layout:

Side bar - by default, when data tables are on the left side, code is on the right side. It is good for Inline diff.

Or top-down layout - when code is below data tables and enough wide to show 2-pane diff.

We can hide tables in any layout by pressing toggle button "Maximize view" to explore code  and return back by pressing again toogle button "Standard View".

Almost all initial setting we can change by toogle buttons panel.

Show Diff - html diff view

Show Vers - usual GUI  code view for a single version.

Compact - show only changes

Full - show all lines + diff

The Blame function is useful for identifying the authors of changes.  Switched off by default as it is a bit time consuming.

By default we can compare double-clicked version with the previous one. But If we want to compare any version we should toogle Diff Pref to Diff any

choose a Base version and  press "Set Base" button,  then double-click any version to compare with the base one.

 

For example base version 16 was compared with v12.

So anyone can use/test this Version explorer. In case there is no AbapGit just copy and paste standalone version.

And as a bonus HTML/JS local comparer )

entry.title: Re: NLQ in the real world: what metadata setup made it successful for you?
entry.content:
Natural language queries have been something business people have dreamed about ever since computers have existed! But of course it turns out that it's WAY harder than anybody initially imaged.

It's fun to note that SQL itself was first promoted as an easy-to-use, business-friendly way of accessing data ("select customer_first_name from customer_table where id=12" is pretty easy, right?!).  

Since then, there have been many, many attempts over the years to bridge the gap between how people ask questions, and the complex code that is required to go and get that data. BusinessObjects Universes were one, of course -- the first "semantic layer" approach.

Straightforward questions work well -- if the metadata is up to date, helping avoid the problem of ambiguity and context -- different parts of the business use the same business term to mean different things. e.g. Headcount in an office means different things if you're talking about actual people right now in the building in case of fire vs. the total payroll. The latest products can spot this and use the user's profile to make assumptions, or ask follow up questions, ("did you mean...?").

But a trickier one is simple questions that require complicated processing to get the right answer -- e.g. "show me the top three salespeople in our most profitable branch office"' could require a cascade of calculations depending on how the system is set up. Now with AI having the ability to create "real unique code" on the fly, this is the next big barrier that might fall -- but so far, reliability is still a question mark.

And then there's the very real problem of uncertainty. There are lots of "simple" business questions where the answer is unknowable and/or requires extrapolation and judgement ("what will the revenue be next quarter?"). There are organizations that are trying to use the most advanced AI to gather lots of information (including external -- the economy, politics, storms, etc.) and come up with the kinds of models that human planners might put together.

entry.title: What is a SAP currency conversion?
entry.content:
If you read my previous article on SAP plant reallocation, you will have seen how political, economic and global change and uncertainty drive the requirement for complex technical change in SAP customers' ERP systems. 

Over the past few years, organisations have had to navigate inflation, volatile exchange rates, geopolitical instability and significant economic uncertainty. In some markets, businesses have faced sustained pressure from weakening local currencies. In others, governments have introduced entirely new currencies or announced plans to adopt new ones. Bulgaria’s planned adoption of the Euro is a good example of how these changes can quickly move from political discussion to operational reality.

When these events happen, businesses need to respond quickly. This often creates a very specific SAP challenge. How do you change the currency structure of an SAP system that may have been operating in the same currency for years?

At first glance, this may not sound particularly challenging. SAP already supports multiple currencies and most organisations regularly process transactions in foreign currencies. However, changing the actual company code currency is a very different challenge altogether. Once a company code has been operating for years, its currency becomes deeply embedded within financial processes, transactional records and reporting structures.

This is where SAP currency conversion comes in.

What is SAP currency conversion?

At a high level, SAP currency conversion refers to changing the local currency of a company code and converting the associated financial and transactional data so that the business can continue operating under a new currency structure.

This may happen because a country adopts a new national currency, because a business wants to move away from an unstable local currency or because organisational restructuring requires financial realignment across different currencies.

Regardless of the reason, the challenge tends to be the same. The business needs to move to a new currency structure. Your SAP ERP system, however, still contains years of financial history built around the old one. This means organisations cannot simply change a configuration setting and move on.

A company code currency is tied to general ledger balances, open receivables, open payables, asset accounting records, controlling objects and historical financial transactions. Over time, this creates a significant amount of dependency across the system. When the Eurozone countries moved to the Euro in 1999, the average SAP system would have been relatively small. Over the past quarter of a century, it's not unusual for SAP production systems to be 5TB and have billions of lines of transactional data. Some large ERP systems are ten times that size.

Why is this difficult in SAP?

If an organisation simply changes the currency setting at company code level, historical transactions remain stored in the original currency while new transactions begin posting in the new one. That quickly creates inconsistencies across financial reporting, reconciliation processes and audit requirements.

Businesses still need access to historical financial records and finance teams need confidence that reporting remains accurate before and after the conversion. Asset values need to remain consistent, open items need to be handled correctly and controlling structures need to continue functioning as expected. Integrations with downstream systems may also need to be reviewed.

Like many SAP transformation challenges, the configuration change itself is often relatively small. The real complexity sits within the underlying data.

The traditional approach

Historically, organisations often approached currency conversion through manual restructuring.

This might involve creating new company codes, migrating selected balances, manually adjusting financial records and maintaining historical environments for reporting purposes. Perhaps using application-level migration methods like BAPIs via a tool like LSMW,

While this approach can work, it often creates significant operational overhead. Finance teams may spend large amounts of time reconciling balances and manually validating financial outputs. And from the perspective of the business user, it creates a break between the transactional activity in the new company and the historical activity in the previous company code, meaning parallel reporting structures for at least a year or two.

The traditional approach often solves the immediate issue, but it is difficult and creates perhaps unnecessary complexity in the process. In some cases, organisations even delay the conversion because the perceived risk feels too high.

The table-based transformation approach

A more modern approach involves transforming the underlying SAP data model at table level rather than manually rebuilding financial structures from scratch.

Instead of creating entirely new company codes and manually recreating financial records, organisations can use specialist landscape transformation technology such as Natuvion DCS to convert the relevant SAP data directly. This allows the existing company code structure to remain in place while the underlying financial data is converted to reflect the new currency model.

This typically includes converting company code currency assignments, general ledger balances, open items, asset accounting data, controlling objects and related financial records. Because these data objects are heavily interconnected, this requires a highly structured approach that ensures consistency across all affected tables.

Landscape transformation software enables organisations to analyse impacted tables, identify dependencies and execute the conversion in a controlled way while preserving historical financial integrity. Rather than manually rebuilding financial structures, organisations can convert existing data with significantly less disruption to day-to-day operations.

This can reduce downtime, minimise reconciliation effort and allow organisations to respond much faster when economic conditions create urgent change.

Final thoughts

Currency conversion may not be discussed as frequently as other SAP project types eg S/4HANA migration, but it is becoming increasingly relevant as businesses navigate economic volatility, regulatory change and global uncertainty.

The challenge is that what initially sounds like a simple finance decision can quickly become a major SAP transformation project if approached incorrectly. Traditional methods can still work, but they are often slower, more manual and far more disruptive than organisations expect.

Table-based transformation approaches are giving businesses a far more efficient way to adapt when currency structures need to change, while preserving the financial integrity that organisations depend on.

If you would like to read more on this topic, you can do so here.

SAP Demand Driven (MRP) - Optimize Inventory Level and Secure Customer serviceSAP Demand-Driven MRP (DDMRP) is an advanced supply chain planning approach designed to optimize inventory levels while ensuring high customer service levels. It helps businesses manage material flow more efficiently by responding to real demand signals rather than relying solely on traditional forecast-based planning.Key Features & Benefits:Demand-Driven Buffer Management:Uses strategic decoupling points in the supply chain to buffer inventory where needed.Helps reduce lead times and prevent stockouts or excess inventory.Optimized Inventory Levels:Avoids the bullwhip effect by dynamically adjusting inventory positions.Reduces working capital while ensuring product availability.Improved Customer Service:Aligns production and supply with actual market demand.Ensures on-time delivery by maintaining optimal stock levels.Adaptive Planning & Execution:Incorporates real-time demand signals to drive production and replenishment.Uses color-coded buffer zones (green, yellow, red) for inventory control.

It enables accurate reporting, seamless revaluation, and effective transaction management across currencies. This enhances financial transparency and minimizes risks from currency fluctuations. ​

Cash ManagementSAP S/4HANA Cash Management provides real-time financial data for accurate cash flow monitoring and decision-making. This ensures that debits and credits across GL accounts remain equal while meeting reporting requirements. Verifying General EntriesThe SAP workflow for verifying general journal entries ensures accuracy before posting by involving specific roles. Requesters create and submit entries, while processors verify and approve them within the system. This process enhances control and accountability in financial data management. ​

SAP Digital AccessDigital Access Evaluation Service is one of various services provided by SAP's Global Adoption Insights & License Compliance (GLAC). With this free-of-charge service, SAP offers their customers support to determine the estimated initial number of digital access documents.Direct human access to the Digital Core systems (ERP & SAP S/4HANA) will continue to be licensed on the number of users, while according to SAP's Digital Access pricing model, all indirect access will be charged based on the number of documents associated to nine specific Document types.Based on a detailed evaluation of the relevant document types, customers will have a basis for the decision whether SAP Digital Access is the suitable license model for the future. The result can be used as a baseline for the Digital Access Adoption Program.

What Makes It Interesting

  • No upfront cost: Free trials lower the barrier to entry.
  • Enterprise relevance: Skills learned here are directly applicable to real-world corporate environments.
  • Cutting-edge tech: Access to AI, cloud, and advanced UI frameworks.
  • Community & support: SAP provides documentation, forums, and updates to help developers succeed.

entry.title: Create New Catalog, Space, Page, Section and Role – S4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Like my previous article on classic concept of Fiori Groups in combination with Catalogs, this article is based on single-functional consultant approach where FICO consultants don't have to depend on ABAP or BASIS consultants to achieve overall objective.

We are going to develop a unified Fiori Space, create its relevant page, add related section therein, and then finally assign the respective Fiori tiles / apps.

For smoother learning and easier follow-up, I have devided this article in five main areas:

A. Create New Transport Request 

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

So lets start;

A. Create New Transport Request

Go to t-code SE10 and create a new TR where all configuration activities will be saved.

Click  button

Chose the radio button “Customizing Request”

New Transport Request will be created.

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

  1. In GUI, enter t-code /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST

Following window will open in the browser, this is SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer.

  1. We will create catalog for GL Report F2217 (Display Line Items in General Ledger)

Go to SAP online Fiori Library https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#

and find the app F2217

Go to Tab  and expand the node ‘Configuration’

Find the catalog (either technical or business),

I chose the technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

  1. Go back to FLP Designer page (shown in step B-1).

Click on button available on right top corner of FLP Designer screen.

Following pop-up window will appear, here select the Transport Request you created in step A.

Click OK button to close the pop-up window. Now all activities will save in this transport request.

  1. Click on button available on left bottom of the screen.

A pop-up window will appear, here provide the Title and ID of new catalog.

Click on save button to create our new catalog.

  1. Now in catalog search field, specify the standard technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

Here find the app F2217

Click on the button “Tiles” as shown below:

Tile details will appear in table as below:

Select the row and you will notice few buttons have been enabled in bottom tray.

One of these isclick it, and following pop-up window will show:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of desired tile F2217 in our new catalog.

  1. Now on same screen, select the button “Target Mapping”

Here select the appropriate Semantic Object “GLAccount” and Action “displayGLLineItemReportingView” as shown below:

Now click on buttonand following pop-up window will appear:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of Target Mapping in our new catalog.

Now specify the new Catalog ID in search field (as shown below):

Click on Tile and Target Mapping button to confirm the assignment of same semantic object and action.

This confirms the Tile mapping and Target mapping.

We have completed the creation of new Catalog and it’s mapping with the required app F2217.

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

  1. Go to t-code PFCG and create new role ZFI_GL_REPORTS by clicking the button

     

  2. Click on tab Select Transaction -> SAP Fiori Launchpad -> Launchpad Catalog

     

Following pop-up window will appear,

Here specify the created new catalog ZFI_GL_REPORTS to assign with the role.

Save the data and notice the new catalog assignment.

This completes the catalog assignment to the role.

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

  1. Create New Space

On Fiori, access the app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”

Click on button

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Space ID, Short description, Space title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton

On following screen, as shown below, click on button.

Space is now created and saved.

  1. Create New Page

Access the Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Following screen will show:

Here click on button.

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Page ID, Short description, Page title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton to create the Page.

Following screen will show:

  1. Create New Section

On Page screen, click on  button.

Following field will appear:

Specify the Section title

Click on button to save the section.

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

  1. In GUI, go to t-code PFCG and select the role ZFI_GL_REPORTS, we already created in step C-1.

Click on button

Go to tab, and access the path as shown below:

On following pop-up window, assign the Space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Now click on save button and Space will be assigned to the role.

  1. Assign Page to Space

In Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”, select the already created space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Now click on  button

List of available pages will show.

Here find our page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Select this page by clicking checkbox and then click on button

Click on button.

Page is now assigned to Space.

  1. Assign Fiori App / Tile to Section

Access the Fiori App “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Select the page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Following screen will appear:

Select the Fiori app “Display Line Items in General Ledger”

and click on  button

Now this Fiori tile will show as assigned to page (as shown below)

Click on  button.

This completes the Fiori tile assignment to Page and section.

Now the required app is visible for user on Fiori interface.

Now you can see the tile under the Space and Page.

Just go to User Homepage and find the Space “GL Reports”

Here you can see the Page and Tile under the same Space.

This concludes the article to buidup the Space, Page, Section and Tile/App assignment.

Hope you have enjoyed the read.

Please do comment for any observations and recommendations.

Best Regards,

entry.title: Re: ABAP Versions Explorer
entry.content:
@yury_sichov   This is a very useful tool — especially for anyone who works across multiple transports, TOCs, or large development teams. The ability to quickly navigate versions, compare changes, and even identify authors through the “Who is Blame” feature is a big productivity boost. The multi‑window approach and the option to switch between inline and 2‑pane diff views also make real code review much easier than the classic SAP GUI versioning tools.

One thing I’m curious about as teams adopt this more widely: Have you noticed any performance considerations when exploring very large objects or classes with many includes and methods?

In some systems, version histories can be quite long, and I’m wondering if there are recommended limits, caching strategies, or settings that help keep the tool responsive.

Would love to hear your experience on this.

entry.title: Re: standard SAP report logic
entry.content:
I was able to sort this out, so sharing what worked for me.

In my case, I needed to reuse the logic from a standard SAP report, but I didn’t want to depend on the ALV layout because that changes during upgrades. The safest approach turned out to be a mix of both ideas:

Use SUBMIT only to reuse the core business logic,

but extract the internal table behind the ALV instead of relying on the ALV output.

Once I switched to reading the internal table directly, the whole thing became stable and upgrade‑safe.

Here’s the small piece of code that worked for me:

 

DATA: lr_data TYPE REF TO data.

"Capture ALV output
cl_salv_bs_runtime_info=>set(
display = abap_false
metadata = abap_false
data = abap_true ).

SUBMIT rsusr002 AND RETURN.

TRY.
cl_salv_bs_runtime_info=>get_data_ref( IMPORTING r_data = lr_data ).
ASSIGN lr_data->* TO <lt_result>.
CATCH cx_salv_bs_sc_runtime_info.
cl_salv_bs_runtime_info=>clear_all( ).
RETURN.
ENDTRY.

cl_salv_bs_runtime_info=>clear_all( ).

"Now <lt_result> contains the internal table from the standard report.

This way I reused SAP’s logic without rewriting it, but avoided tying my custom program to the ALV structure. For anything where SAP provides a CDS view or API, I now use those first — they’re cleaner and more stable.

Hope this helps someone running into the same question.

entry.title: Re: Req Object is not getting passed to bound action
entry.content:
The Fix: Normalize the Handler
The arguments you're seeing (entityDefinition, a, c, d, b) suggest the event is being emitted in a way that bypasses the standard action signature. To force it back to the req object, use an explicit arrow function in your init():

JavaScript
init() {
this.on('doSomethingA', 'A', req => this.doSomethingA(req));
// Use the arrow function to ensure 'req' is the first and only argument passed
this.on('doSomethingB', 'B', req => this.doSomethingB(req));
return super.init();
}

async doSomethingB(req) {
// Now req.params and req.subject will be available
const { ID } = req.params[0];
}

I feel this all happen because : -
Event Bubbling: If B is a composition of another entity, CAP sometimes emits "internal" events for the path navigation. Using the (req) => ... wrapper forces the handler to catch the actual OData request context.

Missing super.init(): Ensure you are returning super.init() at the end of your init() function, or the service lifecycle might behave inconsistently.

The URL Path: Ensure you are calling the action via the bound path: /B(ID=...)/doSomethingB. If the URL is slightly off, CAP might treat it as a generic call where the arguments get messy.

Try the arrow function approach first—it's the most reliable way to "reset" the argument signature to the standard req object.

entry.title: Re: ABAP Versions Explorer
entry.content: @Atul_Joshi85 Thanks! Of course performance probably could be better. (Diff was developed by Claude Opus/Sonnet 4.6) For code size about 10 thousands row diff build can take about several minutes especially with Blame checkbox. And the other porblem - is a large html also take time to render. So for objects > 10 000 rows onlt "Compact" view (only diff) is possible. For classes there are no problems as all methods are in separate includes. Performance issues can be only for standalones programs wihout includes.

entry.title: Create New Catalog, Space, Page, Section and Role – S4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Like my previous article on classic concept of Fiori Groups in combination with Catalogs, this article is based on single-functional consultant approach where FICO consultants don't have to depend on ABAP or BASIS consultants to achieve overall objective.

We are going to develop a unified Fiori Space, create its relevant page, add related section therein, and then finally assign the respective Fiori tiles / apps.

For smoother learning and easier follow-up, I have devided this article in five main areas:

A. Create New Transport Request 

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

So lets start;

A. Create New Transport Request

Go to t-code SE10 and create a new TR where all configuration activities will be saved.

Click  button

Chose the radio button “Customizing Request”

New Transport Request will be created.

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

  1. In GUI, enter t-code /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST

Following window will open in the browser, this is SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer.

  1. We will create catalog for GL Report F2217 (Display Line Items in General Ledger)

Go to SAP online Fiori Library https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#

and find the app F2217

Go to Tab  and expand the node ‘Configuration’

Find the catalog (either technical or business),

I chose the technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

  1. Go back to FLP Designer page (shown in step B-1).

Click on button available on right top corner of FLP Designer screen.

Following pop-up window will appear, here select the Transport Request you created in step A.

Click OK button to close the pop-up window. Now all activities will save in this transport request.

  1. Click on button available on left bottom of the screen.

A pop-up window will appear, here provide the Title and ID of new catalog.

Click on save button to create our new catalog.

  1. Now in catalog search field, specify the standard technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

Here find the app F2217

Click on the button “Tiles” as shown below:

Tile details will appear in table as below:

Select the row and you will notice few buttons have been enabled in bottom tray.

One of these isclick it, and following pop-up window will show:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of desired tile F2217 in our new catalog.

  1. Now on same screen, select the button “Target Mapping”

Here select the appropriate Semantic Object “GLAccount” and Action “displayGLLineItemReportingView” as shown below:

Now click on buttonand following pop-up window will appear:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of Target Mapping in our new catalog.

Now specify the new Catalog ID in search field (as shown below):

Click on Tile and Target Mapping button to confirm the assignment of same semantic object and action.

This confirms the Tile mapping and Target mapping.

We have completed the creation of new Catalog and it’s mapping with the required app F2217.

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

  1. Go to t-code PFCG and create new role ZFI_GL_REPORTS by clicking the button

     

  2. Click on tab Select Transaction -> SAP Fiori Launchpad -> Launchpad Catalog

     

Following pop-up window will appear,

Here specify the created new catalog ZFI_GL_REPORTS to assign with the role.

Save the data and notice the new catalog assignment.

This completes the catalog assignment to the role.

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

  1. Create New Space

On Fiori, access the app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”

Click on button

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Space ID, Short description, Space title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton

On following screen, as shown below, click on button.

Space is now created and saved.

  1. Create New Page

Access the Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Following screen will show:

Here click on button.

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Page ID, Short description, Page title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton to create the Page.

Following screen will show:

  1. Create New Section

On Page screen, click on  button.

Following field will appear:

Specify the Section title

Click on button to save the section.

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

  1. In GUI, go to t-code PFCG and select the role ZFI_GL_REPORTS, we already created in step C-1.

Click on button

Go to tab, and access the path as shown below:

On following pop-up window, assign the Space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Now click on save button and Space will be assigned to the role.

  1. Assign Page to Space

In Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”, select the already created space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Now click on  button

List of available pages will show.

Here find our page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Select this page by clicking checkbox and then click on button

Click on button.

Page is now assigned to Space.

  1. Assign Fiori App / Tile to Section

Access the Fiori App “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Select the page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Following screen will appear:

Select the Fiori app “Display Line Items in General Ledger”

and click on  button

Now this Fiori tile will show as assigned to page (as shown below)

Click on  button.

This completes the Fiori tile assignment to Page and section.

Now the required app is visible for user on Fiori interface.

Now you can see the tile under the Space and Page.

Just go to User Homepage and find the Space “GL Reports”

Here you can see the Page and Tile under the same Space.

This concludes the article to buidup the Space, Page, Section and Tile/App assignment.

Hope you have enjoyed the read.

Please do comment for any observations and recommendations.

Best Regards,

entry.title: Re: ABAP Versions Explorer
entry.content: Added: Per-instance cache for rendered diff HTML. Key: object type + name + versno old/new + flags (blame, two_pane, compact, debug). On hit — returns stored HTML immediately, skipping source load, diff and blame computation. On miss — computes as usual and stores the result. Cache is invalidated automatically when the popup window closes.

entry.title: Re: Asset data migration inconsistent
entry.content:
Hi 

 

The discrepancy you are seeing moving from a flat 2  (9,975.96 EUR) to 2.05  (10,207.95 EUR) is a classic behavior in SAP S/4HANA Asset Accounting during mid-year migrations. The system is likely not ignoring your 2 %  configuration rather, it is performing a Catch Up or Smoothing calculation based on the remaining net book value and the remaining useful life.

The Remaining Life Calculation Logic In S/4HANA, when you migrate an asset mid-year, the system validates the Net Book Value  against the Remaining Useful Life. Total Life: 50 Years. Expired Life at Transfer 28 Years, 6 Months. Remaining Useful Life: 21 Years, 6 Months or 21.5 years. If the system is set to calculate depreciation based on the remaining life and the remaining value, the math looks like this

Annual Depreciation = NBV at Transfer\Remaining Life

Annual Depreciation = 219,471.01  - 4,987.98  \ 21.0

However, if we look at your specific calculation  219,471.01 \ 21.5  = 10,207.95

This matches your "incorrect" value exactly. The system is taking the NBV as of the start of the year and dividing it by the remaining life as of that same date to ensure the asset fully depreciates to zero by the end of the 50-year term.

The National Code 2015 2% is likely a Straight Line from APC key. However, when you migrated the asset, the accumulated depreciation you entered 279,326.89 might not perfectly align with a mathematical 2% per year since 1998.

If there is even a small rounding difference in the legacy accumulated depreciation, SAP corrects the rate for the remaining periods to ensure the asset reaches a zero book value exactly at the end of year 50.

To bring this back to the expected 9,975.96 check  in Transaction AFAMP Change Period Control Methods and AFAMA Depreciation Keys, Depreciation Calculation Method, Check if the Multi-Level Method is set to calculate based on Remaining Life instead of Installation Date.  If it is set to Remaining Life, SAP will always adjust the percentage to fit the remaining value. Smoothing vs. Catch-up: In the Company Code settings for Asset Accounting, check if Smoothing is active. PT04 is custom right.

entry.title: Re: Managing Themes Across SAP Environments
entry.content:
Hi @barbara_hartel ,

I would suggest that you link to the Blog post from @andreas-roessler who provides the details how a Theme can be transported between Subaccounts using the SAP Content Agent Service: Effortlessly Sharing Themes Across Your SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Environments.

Best regards,
Gregor

entry.title: Re: Effortlessly Sharing Themes Across Your SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Environments
entry.content:
Hi @andreas-roessler ,

I can't agree with the "well-explaine" in the documentation. I just had to setup SAP Content Agent Service and unfortunately it's a lot of try and error. I haven't found any mentioining of the Content Type that needs to be configured in the Transport Node. The correct one is "Multi-Target Application". Also I would suggest that you re-draw the picture in this post and increase the Subaccount box also arround Theming and CAS. The CAS Instance must be subscribed in the Subaccount where the Theme Designer is used. And the same needs to be applied for the Target Landscape where not only CAS needs to be subscribed but also an instance of the CAS with the Plan: application must be created. The service Key of this instance must be used to create the destination in the cTMS Subaccount.

Best regards,
Gregor

entry.title: Product Compliance / About content and what you may be expect to get
entry.content:
Introduction

We can see many Blogs regarding "Content" in Product Compliance.

First: we need to define: is there a difference regarding "Content" in Product Compliance compared to SAP EHS classic?

The answer is: partielly there is a difference. 

First you need to understand: what content comes via SAP and what content not.

And we need to define the "content".

In old days using 3E as an example content can be defined as:

  • you are getting new phrases or new phrase translations
  • you are getting new rule sets or updates of rule sets
  • you get new WWI templates and updates of WWI templates
  • you get new legal contetent or updates on legal content (including e.g. Dangerous goods content)
  • you get an update of property trees

Using this definition of content: this is now different (based on best knowledge)

On long term you are getting updates of compliance requirement and/or legal content via SAP.  This can include: new phrases and new translations of phrases and include new "CAS" numbers and update of legal content for the "CAS" numbers.

What is the same.. SAP (best knowledge) will not deliver "rulesets"  or "WWI" like templates. Here you are free to select a content provider.

What will be as well different: User Experience is different... And this can help to manage the content.

SAP plans do delivery "AI" and agents so what data maintenance can be supported. This is a new functionality not available wirh EHS classic.  What exactly we will get.. we need to wait. But this is a kind of new functionality and to a certain extent you might call this a new content. 

But we need to wait at laetss for on premise version 2027 (or a clod version) to see; what is delivered, how flexible this might be and to whcih extent the new content will help.

Links
